summary:
South Sudan's path to independence stretched across the second half of the twentieth century through two devastating civil wars before formal independence was achieved in 2011. The first Sudanese civil war (1955–1972) and the second (1983–2005) pitted the predominantly Christian and animist population of the south against the Arab Muslim government in Khartoum, which sought to impose Islamic law nationwide. The wars claimed an estimated two million lives in the second conflict alone, primarily through famine, disease, and displacement.

The Comprehensive Peace Agreement of 2005 ended the second civil war and granted the south six years of autonomous rule followed by a referendum on independence. John Garang, leader of the Sudan People's Liberation Movement, was killed in a helicopter crash weeks after the agreement was signed. The January 2011 referendum returned an overwhelming vote — over 98 percent — in favor of independence, and South Sudan formally became the world's newest nation on July 9, 2011. The country's independence was recognized internationally but its post-independence history was marred almost immediately by renewed internal conflict that began in 2013.
